summary: Spolsky debates whether FogBUGZ 3.0 should support custom fields, a feature he has historically opposed. He argues that custom fields lead to bloated bug entry forms that discourage people from reporting bugs at all, which is far worse than missing some metadata. When he surveyed customers requesting custom fields, he found most wanted fields that already existed in the product or fields that shouldn't be tracked as structured data. Despite his conviction that omitting custom fields is the right design choice, he acknowledges the sales cost of appearing to lack a feature competitors offer.
key_points: Adding fields to bug databases increases ceremony and reduces the number of bugs that get reported, which is worse than missing metadata
Most customers requesting custom fields either want something already built-in or want fields that would be counterproductive as structured data
The primary goal of a bug tracker is to keep track of bugs, not to capture exhaustive structured information about them
Information that's only relevant to a small percentage of bugs should be captured on demand rather than required for every report
Good product design sometimes means telling customers 'no' even when it costs sales
money_quotes: People add so many fields to their bug databases to capture everything they think might be important that entering a bug is like applying to Harvard.
End result: people don't enter bugs, which is much, much worse than not capturing all that information.
The goal of a bug tracking database is to keep track of bugs, which, all else being equal, takes priority over making it easy to find them.

I have heard countless stories of development teams where the bug tracking package was so high-ceremony that people were afraid to enter bugs in the system, because they didn't know what all those fields were.
The real bug-'tracking' happened in email, post-its, and hallway conversations.
Rather than giving our usual answer ('no. on purpose.') over the last few weeks I've been saying, 'can you please tell me what fields you would need?'
It's still rude of me to tell customers that we don't have that feature for their own good, even though it usually is.
I guess we could have a custom fields feature but hide it and make it so hard to use that people don't use it.
key_insight: The best bug tracking tools minimize friction for reporting bugs rather than maximizing the structured data captured per bug, because an unreported bug is far more costly than an under-documented one.
